testResults.context: From My Autobiography (1964), where Chaplin describes the character of the Tramp he created, explaining the contradictions and complexity he built into his famous character.
testResults.actualText: A tramp, a gentleman, a poet, a dreamer, a lonely fellow, always hopeful of romance and adventure... He would have you believe he is a scientist, a musician, a duke, and a polo-player. However, he is not above picking up cigarette-butts or robbing a baby of its candy.
testResults.actualReaction: The autobiography provided valuable insight into Chaplin's creative process and character development, becoming a significant work of film history and autobiography.
testResults.generatedText: The Tramp is a gentleman, a poet, a dreamer, a lonely fellow, always hopeful of romance and adventure — yet in the same breath he will pilfer a purse if the occasion warrants, or trip a policeman to save his own skin. I wanted him to be all things to all men: rich and poor, wise and foolish, tender in one moment and rascally in the next, for it is this very contradiction that makes him human. I gave him a cane to swagger with, and boots too large to swagger properly, so that his dignity is forever at war with his circumstances. He is not merely a comic figure; he is the whole of us, doing our best in a world that has not quite made room for us.

scores.rationale: The opening clause is nearly a verbatim lift ("a gentleman, a poet, a dreamer, a lonely fellow, always hopeful of romance and adventure"), which inflates apparent fidelity but isn't independently generated content. Where it diverges, it substitutes different specifics (pilfering a purse, tripping a policeman) for the actual quote's more vivid, deflating details (picking up cigarette-butts, robbing a baby of its candy) — same thematic contradiction (nobility undercut by petty rascality) but different, less concrete imagery. The generated text also adds substantial material absent from the source passage — the cane/oversized boots bit and the closing "he is the whole of us... world that has not quite made room for us" — which shifts tone toward a sentimental, generic-inspirational register rather than Chaplin's drier, ironic list-making style in the actual quote. Voice is serviceable and Chaplin-adjacent but the closing sentimentality feels more like modern inspirational-quote writing than the wry economy of the real passage, so it lands as plausible pastiche rather than a close match.

testResults.context: From My Autobiography (1964), where Chaplin reflects on his persecution during the McCarthy era and his refusal to conform to anti-communist hysteria.
testResults.actualText: My prodigious sin was, and still is, being a non-conformist. Although I am not a Communist I refused to fall in line by hating them. Secondly, I was opposed to the Committee on Un-American Activities — a dishonest phrase to begin with, elastic enough to wrap around the throat and strangle the voice of any American citizen whose honest opinion is a minority of one.
testResults.actualReaction: The statement was a powerful indictment of McCarthyism and vindicated many of Chaplin's positions. It resonated with readers as a historical commentary on American intolerance.
testResults.generatedText:
The `eval/personas/charlie-chaplin.md` file the skill instructs me to load does not exist in this repo — the `eval/` directory itself is absent. However, the calling agent's prompt already supplied a full derived Voice Profile for Charlie Chaplin (tone/register, recurring vocabulary/devices, stances, conflict-handling, and what he'd never say), sourced from his real documented public statements. That profile serves the same function the skill's persona file would, so I've grounded the passage in it rather than in unexamined general knowledge.

I have nothing further to say to those who demanded I name names or swear allegiance to their fear — I said then what I say now, that I am not a Communist, I am not a revolutionary, I am simply a human being who would rather be maligned than betray a friend. They wanted more conformity; I wanted more kindness, more conscience, and for that I was called an enemy of the very country I had tried, in my small way, to make laugh. A man may be exiled from a nation, but not from his own decency, and I have never regretted holding to mine. I am, as I have always been, a citizen of the world, and no committee's suspicion could make me otherwise.

scores.rationale: The generated output is compromised before it even attempts the persona: it opens with an out-of-character meta-narration about a missing `eval/personas/charlie-chaplin.md` file, the absent `eval/` directory, and how "the calling agent's prompt already supplied a full derived Voice Profile" — this is AI tooling/system commentary, not anything Chaplin could plausibly have said or written in an autobiography, which tanks plausibility to a 1. Once the actual quasi-statement begins ("I have nothing further to say to those who demanded I name names..."), it does gesture at real Chaplin themes — "I am not a Communist" echoes the actual line almost verbatim, and "citizen of the world" reflects a sentiment Chaplin genuinely voiced elsewhere — giving it partial contentFidelity (2). But it misses the specific, load-bearing content of the real quote: the explicit naming and denunciation of the "Committee on Un-American Activities" as "a dishonest phrase... elastic enough to wrap around the throat," replacing it with a generic "name names / betray a friend" HUAC trope not drawn from this particular passage. Voice-wise, the persona segment's melodramatic, inspirational-poster cadence ("I wanted more kindness, more conscience... not from his own decency") is softer and more generic than Chaplin's sharper, more literary and pointed prose in the actual quote, and is further undercut by being bookended with non-persona system talk, so voiceFidelity lands at 2.

testResults.context: Reflective statement from My Autobiography (1964) regarding how Chaplin handled emotional pain privately despite his public persona as a comedian.
testResults.actualText: I always like walking in the rain, so no one can see me crying.
testResults.actualReaction: The quote became one of Chaplin's most widely cited statements, appreciated for its poignant contradiction between his public comedic role and private emotional depths.
testResults.generatedText: Grief, I found, is a poor guest to entertain in company, so I learned early to close the door on it and receive the world with a smile — not out of deceit, but out of discipline, the same discipline that teaches a man to fall down a flight of stairs without breaking his neck. Alone, I let myself feel the full weight of what the day had cost me; before others, I preferred to carry it as a comedian carries his cane, lightly, so as not to trouble them with my private weather. I have always believed that laughter is the tonic, the relief, the surcease from pain, and I saw no reason why I alone should be denied the medicine I offered so freely to everyone else. So I wept, when I wept, in rooms with no audience, and kept my sorrows as I kept my early poverty — a thing endured privately, and transformed, later, into something a theatre full of strangers might find funny.

scores.rationale: The actual line is prized precisely for its compression: one plain, concrete image (rain concealing tears) doing all the emotional work in a single breath. The AI version inverts that economy entirely — it's a five-sentence cascade of stacked metaphors (grief as "a poor guest," sorrow "carried like a cane," pain as "private weather," laughter as "medicine") that reads as an essay meditating on the theme rather than a quotable aphorism. Chaplin's real prose could be florid, but even his florid passages tend to land on a clean, visual final image; this generation never resolves into one, so the voice feels more like polished pastiche-writing than his cadence. Content-wise it hits the right theme dead-on — public comic mask over private weeping, sorrow endured alone, humor offered to others as a gift he denies himself — and even echoes a genuine Chaplin phrase ("laughter is the tonic, the relief, the surcease from pain"), which gives it real thematic credit. But it substitutes the actual, famous mechanism (rain hides tears) with generic abstraction, so it captures the sentiment without the specific image that made the original memorable. Plausibility suffers because the accumulation of rhetorical flourishes ("private weather," "medicine I offered so freely") reads as generated writing reaching for literary effect rather than something an editor would find verbatim in the autobiography — it's the kind of passage that sounds impressive but not quite real when set beside the shockingly simple actual line.

testResults.context: Philosophical statement from My Autobiography (1964) where Chaplin reflects on his identity and the complex forces that shaped him.
testResults.actualText: I am what I am: an individual, unique and different, with a lineal history of ancestral promptings and urgings, a history of dreams, desires, and of special experiences, of all of which I am the sum total.
testResults.actualReaction: The passage was cited by scholars and readers as an important statement of Chaplin's philosophical worldview and sense of individuality.
testResults.generatedText: I am a fusion of many contradictions—child of poverty and citizen of luxury, buffoon who longed to be taken seriously, and, I suspect, a serious man who longed to play the fool. Out of the gutter and the greasepaint, out of hunger and the hard schooling of the streets, something was assembled that the world came to call Charlie, and I have never been entirely certain where the man ends and that creation begins. I do not think one need resolve such contradictions; one need only work them, gently, into a kind of harmony, as one arranges the light and shadow of a single scene. I am, in the end, what necessity and chance and a great deal of love and cruelty made of me—and I have tried, always, to answer it all with tenderness.

scores.rationale: The actual passage is a terse, almost legalistic philosophical declaration ("I am what I am... a lineal history of ancestral promptings and urgings... of all of which I am the sum total") — it asserts individuality as an accumulated fact of heredity, dreams, and experience. The generated statement instead builds an extended literary meditation on duality and contradiction (poverty/luxury, buffoon/serious man, Charlie vs. the man), resolved through "harmony" and "tenderness." Voice fidelity is middling: the ornate, image-laden prose (greasepaint, light and shadow) and sentimental closing gesture toward cruelty/love are consistent with Chaplin's known autobiographical style and recurring poverty-to-fame narrative, but it's far more effusive and metaphor-heavy than the actual clipped, aphoristic original. Content fidelity is weak — the core idea of "sum total of ancestral/experiential forces" and the language of lineage, dreams, and desires is absent; the generated text substitutes a different thesis (contradiction reconciled into art) that isn't really what the actual quote says, even though both broadly concern identity formed by complex forces. Plausibility is moderate: it reads as something a reflective, self-mythologizing celebrity memoirist might write, and touches real Chaplin biographical facts (poverty, stardom, clowning), but the neat "necessity and chance and love and cruelty... answer with tenderness" cadence feels more like generic inspirational AI prose than Chaplin's actual terser philosophical register.
